As mentioned above, ''shichidō garan'' could mean a complete temple or even simply a large temple complex. According to a 13th-century text,[[Shōtoku Taishi]] Denkokonmokurokushō (聖徳太子伝古今目録抄) "a garan is a temple with a ''hon-dō'' (main hall), a ''[[tō]]'' (pagoda), a ''kō-dō'' (lecture hall), a ''[[shōrō]]'' (belfry), a ''jiki-dō'' (refectory), a ''sōbō'' (monks' living quarters), and a ''[[kyōzō]]'' (scriptures deposit, library)." These are the seven listed as ''shichidō'' elements of a {{nihongo|[[Buddhism in Japan#Asuka and Nara Periods|Nanto Rokushū]]|南都六宗|Nara six sects}}The six Buddhist schools {{nihongo2|南都六宗}}, introduced to Japan during the [[Asuka Period|Asuka]] and [[Nara period]]s temple.Iwanami [[Kōjien]] A 15th-century text{{nihongo|Sekiso Ōrai|尺素往来}} describes how [[Zen]] school temples ([[Sōtō]] ({{lang|ja|曹洞}}), [[Rinzai]] ({{lang|ja|臨済}}))The [[Ōbaku|Ōbaku School]] ({{lang|ja|黃檗}}) arrived in Japan in the 17th century. included a ''[[butsuden]]'' or ''butsu-dō'' (main hall), a ''hattō'' (lecture hall), a ''kuin'' (kitchen/office), a ''sō-dō'' (building dedicated to [[Zazen]]), a ''[[sanmon]]'' (main gate), a ''tōsu'' (toilet) and a ''yokushitsu'' (bath). ===Loss of importance of the pagoda within the ''garan''=== [[File:飛鳥寺復元図.jpg|thumb|left|A reconstruction of [[Asuka-dera]]'s original layout with a pagoda at its center]] Because of the relics they contained, wooden pagodas used to be the centerpiece of the ''garan'', the seven edifices considered indispensable for a temple.*{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |edition=First |pages=40–41 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} They gradually lost importance and were replaced by the ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|kondō]]'' (golden hall), because of the magic powers believed to lie within the images the building housed. This loss of status was so complete that the Zen sects, which arrived late in Japan from China, normally do not build any pagoda at all. The layout of four early temples clearly illustrates this trend: they are in chronological order [[Asuka-dera]], [[Shitennō-ji]], [[Hōryū-ji]], and [[Yakushi-ji]]. In the first, the pagoda was at the very center of the ''garan'' surrounded by three small ''kondō'' (see the reconstruction of the temple's original layout). In the second, a single ''kondō'' is at the center of the temple and the pagoda lies in front of it. At Hōryū-ji, they are one next to the other. Yakushi-ji has a single, large ''kondō'' at the center with two pagodas on the sides. The same evolution can be observed in Buddhist temples in China. ==Examples of ''garan''== ===Hōryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Horyuji Temple.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Hōryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] {{nihongo|Hōryū-ji|法隆寺}} is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple of the "[[Shōtoku (sect)|Shōtoku]]" sect in [[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. Its ''garan'' is composed of (see plan on the right): A ''Chū[[Mon (architecture)|mon]]'' (中門)
In a temple, the gate after the ''naindaimon'' connected to a ''kairō'' B ''[[Kairō]]'' (回廊・廻廊)
A long and roofed [[portico]]-like passage surrounding the ''kondō'' and the pagoda.JAANUS entry of the same name C ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kon-dō]]'' (金堂)
The main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship. D ''[[Tō]]''
A pagoda, which is an evolution of the [[stupa]] (a kind of [[reliquary]]) . After reaching China, the stupa evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen). E ''Kōdō'' (講堂)
The lecture hall of a non-Zen ''garan''. F ''[[Kyōzō]]'' (経蔵)
Lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of [[sūtra]]s and books about the temple's history. Also called '''kyōdō'''. G ''[[Shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)
A belfry ===Zuiryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Zuiryuji.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Zuiryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] [[Zuiryū-ji (Toyama)|Zuiryū-ji]] is a Zen temple of the [[Sōtō]] sect in [[Takaoka, Toyama|Takaoka]], [[Toyama Prefecture]]. A ''[[Sōmon]]'' (総門)
The gate at the entrance of a temple. It precedes the bigger and more important ''sanmon''. B ''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門 or 山門)
The gate in front of the '''butsuden''', most commonly having two stories ([[nijūmon]]). The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''gate of the three liberations''. Its openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment. Entering, one can symbolically free him or herself from the three passions of {{nihongo|ton|貪| greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009 C ''Kairō'' (回廊)
See above D ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Butsuden]]'' (仏殿)
Lit. "Hall of Buddha". A building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a ''bodhisattva'' and dedicated to prayer. E ''Hōdō'' (法堂)
Lit. "[[Dharma]] hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the '''hō'''). F ''Zendō'' (禅堂)
Lit. "hall of Zen". The building where monks practice '''zazen''', and one of the main structures of a Zen '''garan'''. G ''Shōrō'' (鐘楼)
A belfry H ''Kuri'' (庫裏)
A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a Zen ''garan''. Another typical Zen ''garan'', of which [[Kenchō-ji]]'s is a good example, begins with the ''sōmon'' followed by the ''sanmon'', the main hall (the ''[[butsuden]]''), the lecture hall (''hattō''), and the chief abbot's residence (''hōjō'') all aligned more or less on a north to south axis, with the bath house (''yokushitsu'') and the [[sūtra]] repository (''[[kyōzō]]'') to its east, and the monks' hall (''sodō'') to its west. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Notes== ==References== * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 27, 2009 * Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{commons Category|Shichidō garan}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shichido garan}} [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shichidō garan]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shichidō garan]][[Category:qq]]

{{short description|Japanese Buddhist term}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2014}} {{redirect|Garan|people named Garan|Garan (name)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref04s5s4290.jpg|thumb|300px|Part of [[Tōshōdai-ji]]'s ''garan'' (left to right, the ''kondō'', the ''kōdō'', the ''korō'' and the ''raidō'')]] '''''Shichidō garan''''' is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] term indicating the seven [[Dō (architecture)|halls]] composing the ideal Buddhist temple compound. This compound word is composed of {{nihongo|''shichidō''|七堂|}}, literally meaning "seven halls", and {{nihongo|''garan''|伽藍}}, meaning "temple". The term is often shortened to just ''garan''. Which seven halls the term refers to varies, and 七堂 may be a misinterpretation of {{nihongo|''shitsudō''|悉堂}}, meaning "complete temple".Iwanami [[Kōjien]]Kōsetsu Bukkyō Daijiten (広説仏教語大辞典)The Japanese character 七 can also be pronounced ''shitsu''. In practice, ''shichidō garan'' often simply means a large temple with many buildings.{{cite book | last = Imaizumi | first = Yoshio | title = Nihon Bukkyo Shi Jiten | publisher = Yoshikawa Kobunkan | location = City | year = 1999 | isbn = 978-4-642-01334-5 }} ==Etymology and history of the term== {{nihongo|''Garan''|伽藍}}Also called {{nihongo|sōen|僧園}} {{nihongo|shūen|衆園}} and {{nihongo|shōja|精舎}} in Japanese is an abbreviated form of the expression {{nihongo|''sōgya ranma''|僧伽藍摩}}, itself a transliteration of the Sanskrit ''saMghaaraama'' (सँघाराम), literally meaning "garden for monks".JAANUS, ''garan'' A Japanese ''garan'' was originally just a park where monks gathered together with their teacher, but the term later came to mean "[[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]]". The word ''garan'' can be found in a record in [[Nihon Shoki]] dated 552, although no monastery of this time survives, so we don't know what they were like. The compound word {{nihongo|''shichidō garan''|七堂伽藍|seven hall temple}} is found in a much more recent literature of [[Edo period]],[[Nihon Kokugo Daijiten]] referring to a complex that had a complete set of buildings forming an ideal Buddhist temple. == Early ''garan'' layout == A record dated 577 in the [[Nihon Shoki]] states that a mission including among others a temple architect and a Buddhist image maker was sent by {{nihongo|[[Seong of Baekje|King Seong of Baekje]]|聖王}} to Japan, with more Buddhist related artisans sent over in the following years.{{cite book |author=Grayson, James Huntley |title=Korea: a religious history |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |location=London |year=2002 |pages=33 |isbn=0-7007-1605-X }} Excavations carried out between 1979-1980 on the temple site of [[Jeongnimsa]] in [[Buyeo County|Buyeo]], capital of [[Baekje]] from 538 to 663, revealed that the original temple was laid out in a typical north-to-south style with key buildings put on the center axis,{{cite web|url=http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|title=National Research Institute of Cultural Heritage|access-date=2010-06-30|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727120021/http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|archive-date=2011-07-27}} which was an arrangement closely adhered to at [[Shitennō-ji]] in [[Ōsaka]]. ==Composition of a ''shichidō garan''== What is counted in the group of seven buildings, or ''shichidō'', can vary greatly from temple to temple, from sect to sect, and from time to time. As mentioned above, ''shichidō garan'' could mean a complete temple or even simply a large temple complex. According to a 13th-century text,[[Shōtoku Taishi]] Denkokonmokurokushō (聖徳太子伝古今目録抄) "a garan is a temple with a ''hon-dō'' (main hall), a ''[[tō]]'' (pagoda), a ''kō-dō'' (lecture hall), a ''[[shōrō]]'' (belfry), a ''jiki-dō'' (refectory), a ''sōbō'' (monks' living quarters), and a ''[[kyōzō]]'' (scriptures deposit, library)." These are the seven listed as ''shichidō'' elements of a {{nihongo|[[Buddhism in Japan#Asuka and Nara Periods|Nanto Rokushū]]|南都六宗|Nara six sects}}The six Buddhist schools {{nihongo2|南都六宗}}, introduced to Japan during the [[Asuka Period|Asuka]] and [[Nara period]]s temple.Iwanami [[Kōjien]] A 15th-century text{{nihongo|Sekiso Ōrai|尺素往来}} describes how [[Zen]] school temples ([[Sōtō]] ({{lang|ja|曹洞}}), [[Rinzai]] ({{lang|ja|臨済}}))The [[Ōbaku|Ōbaku School]] ({{lang|ja|黃檗}}) arrived in Japan in the 17th century. included a ''[[butsuden]]'' or ''butsu-dō'' (main hall), a ''hattō'' (lecture hall), a ''kuin'' (kitchen/office), a ''sō-dō'' (building dedicated to [[Zazen]]), a ''[[sanmon]]'' (main gate), a ''tōsu'' (toilet) and a ''yokushitsu'' (bath). ===Loss of importance of the pagoda within the ''garan''=== [[File:飛鳥寺復元図.jpg|thumb|left|A reconstruction of [[Asuka-dera]]'s original layout with a pagoda at its center]] Because of the relics they contained, wooden pagodas used to be the centerpiece of the ''garan'', the seven edifices considered indispensable for a temple.*{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |edition=First |pages=40–41 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} They gradually lost importance and were replaced by the ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|kondō]]'' (golden hall), because of the magic powers believed to lie within the images the building housed. This loss of status was so complete that the Zen sects, which arrived late in Japan from China, normally do not build any pagoda at all. The layout of four early temples clearly illustrates this trend: they are in chronological order [[Asuka-dera]], [[Shitennō-ji]], [[Hōryū-ji]], and [[Yakushi-ji]]. In the first, the pagoda was at the very center of the ''garan'' surrounded by three small ''kondō'' (see the reconstruction of the temple's original layout). In the second, a single ''kondō'' is at the center of the temple and the pagoda lies in front of it. At Hōryū-ji, they are one next to the other. Yakushi-ji has a single, large ''kondō'' at the center with two pagodas on the sides. The same evolution can be observed in Buddhist temples in China. ==Examples of ''garan''== ===Hōryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Horyuji Temple.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Hōryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] {{nihongo|Hōryū-ji|法隆寺}} is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple of the "[[Shōtoku (sect)|Shōtoku]]" sect in [[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. Its ''garan'' is composed of (see plan on the right): A ''Chū[[Mon (architecture)|mon]]'' (中門)
In a temple, the gate after the ''naindaimon'' connected to a ''kairō'' B ''[[Kairō]]'' (回廊・廻廊)
A long and roofed [[portico]]-like passage surrounding the ''kondō'' and the pagoda.JAANUS entry of the same name C ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kon-dō]]'' (金堂)
The main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship. D ''[[Tō]]''
A pagoda, which is an evolution of the [[stupa]] (a kind of [[reliquary]]) . After reaching China, the stupa evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen). E ''Kōdō'' (講堂)
The lecture hall of a non-Zen ''garan''. F ''[[Kyōzō]]'' (経蔵)
Lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of [[sūtra]]s and books about the temple's history. Also called '''kyōdō'''. G ''[[Shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)
A belfry ===Zuiryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Zuiryuji.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Zuiryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] [[Zuiryū-ji (Toyama)|Zuiryū-ji]] is a Zen temple of the [[Sōtō]] sect in [[Takaoka, Toyama|Takaoka]], [[Toyama Prefecture]]. A ''[[Sōmon]]'' (総門)
The gate at the entrance of a temple. It precedes the bigger and more important ''sanmon''. B ''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門 or 山門)
The gate in front of the '''butsuden''', most commonly having two stories ([[nijūmon]]). The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''gate of the three liberations''. Its openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment. Entering, one can symbolically free him or herself from the three passions of {{nihongo|ton|貪| greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009 C ''Kairō'' (回廊)
See above D ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Butsuden]]'' (仏殿)
Lit. "Hall of Buddha". A building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a ''bodhisattva'' and dedicated to prayer. E ''Hōdō'' (法堂)
Lit. "[[Dharma]] hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the '''hō'''). F ''Zendō'' (禅堂)
Lit. "hall of Zen". The building where monks practice '''zazen''', and one of the main structures of a Zen '''garan'''. G ''Shōrō'' (鐘楼)
A belfry H ''Kuri'' (庫裏)
A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a Zen ''garan''. Another typical Zen ''garan'', of which [[Kenchō-ji]]'s is a good example, begins with the ''sōmon'' followed by the ''sanmon'', the main hall (the ''[[butsuden]]''), the lecture hall (''hattō''), and the chief abbot's residence (''hōjō'') all aligned more or less on a north to south axis, with the bath house (''yokushitsu'') and the [[sūtra]] repository (''[[kyōzō]]'') to its east, and the monks' hall (''sodō'') to its west. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Notes== ==References== * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 27, 2009 * Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{commons Category|Shichidō garan}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shichido garan}} [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shichidō garan]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shichidō garan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{short description|Japanese Buddhist term}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2014}} {{redirect|Garan|people named Garan|Garan (name)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref04s5s4290.jpg|thumb|300px|Part of [[Tōshōdai-ji]]'s ''garan'' (left to right, the ''kondō'', the ''kōdō'', the ''korō'' and the ''raidō'')]] '''''Shichidō garan''''' is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] term indicating the seven [[Dō (architecture)|halls]] composing the ideal Buddhist temple compound. In the second, a single ''kondō'' is at the center of the temple and the pagoda lies in front of it. At Hōryū-ji, they are one next to the other. Yakushi-ji has a single, large ''kondō'' at the center with two pagodas on the sides. The same evolution can be observed in Buddhist temples in China. ==Examples of ''garan''== ===Hōryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Horyuji Temple.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Hōryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] {{nihongo|Hōryū-ji|法隆寺}} is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple of the "[[Shōtoku (sect)|Shōtoku]]" sect in [[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. Its ''garan'' is composed of (see plan on the right): A ''Chū[[Mon (architecture)|mon]]'' (中門)
In a temple, the gate after the ''naindaimon'' connected to a ''kairō'' B ''[[Kairō]]'' (回廊・廻廊)
A long and roofed [[portico]]-like passage surrounding the ''kondō'' and the pagoda.JAANUS entry of the same name C ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kon-dō]]'' (金堂)
The main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship. D ''[[Tō]]''
A pagoda, which is an evolution of the [[stupa]] (a kind of [[reliquary]]) . After reaching China, the stupa evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen). E ''Kōdō'' (講堂)
The lecture hall of a non-Zen ''garan''. F ''[[Kyōzō]]'' (経蔵)
Lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of [[sūtra]]s and books about the temple's history. Also called '''kyōdō'''. G ''[[Shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)
A belfry ===Zuiryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Zuiryuji.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Zuiryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] [[Zuiryū-ji (Toyama)|Zuiryū-ji]] is a Zen temple of the [[Sōtō]] sect in [[Takaoka, Toyama|Takaoka]], [[Toyama Prefecture]]. A ''[[Sōmon]]'' (総門)
The gate at the entrance of a temple. It precedes the bigger and more important ''sanmon''. B ''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門 or 山門)
The gate in front of the '''butsuden''', most commonly having two stories ([[nijūmon]]). The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''gate of the three liberations''. Its openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment. Entering, one can symbolically free him or herself from the three passions of {{nihongo|ton|貪| greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009 C ''Kairō'' (回廊)
See above D ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Butsuden]]'' (仏殿)
Lit. "Hall of Buddha". A building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a ''bodhisattva'' and dedicated to prayer. E ''Hōdō'' (法堂)
Lit. "[[Dharma]] hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the '''hō'''). F ''Zendō'' (禅堂)
Lit. "hall of Zen". The building where monks practice '''zazen''', and one of the main structures of a Zen '''garan'''. G ''Shōrō'' (鐘楼)
A belfry H ''Kuri'' (庫裏)
A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a Zen ''garan''. Another typical Zen ''garan'', of which [[Kenchō-ji]]'s is a good example, begins with the ''sōmon'' followed by the ''sanmon'', the main hall (the ''[[butsuden]]''), the lecture hall (''hattō''), and the chief abbot's residence (''hōjō'') all aligned more or less on a north to south axis, with the bath house (''yokushitsu'') and the [[sūtra]] repository (''[[kyōzō]]'') to its east, and the monks' hall (''sodō'') to its west. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Notes== ==References== * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 27, 2009 * Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{commons Category|Shichidō garan}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shichido garan}} [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shichidō garan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{short description|Japanese Buddhist term}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2014}} {{redirect|Garan|people named Garan|Garan (name)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref04s5s4290.jpg|thumb|300px|Part of [[Tōshōdai-ji]]'s ''garan'' (left to right, the ''kondō'', the ''kōdō'', the ''korō'' and the ''raidō'')]] '''''Shichidō garan''''' is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] term indicating the seven [[Dō (architecture)|halls]] composing the ideal Buddhist temple compound. This compound word is composed of {{nihongo|''shichidō''|七堂|}}, literally meaning "seven halls", and {{nihongo|''garan''|伽藍}}, meaning "temple". The term is often shortened to just ''garan''. Which seven halls the term refers to varies, and 七堂 may be a misinterpretation of {{nihongo|''shitsudō''|悉堂}}, meaning "complete temple".Iwanami [[Kōjien]]Kōsetsu Bukkyō Daijiten (広説仏教語大辞典)The Japanese character 七 can also be pronounced ''shitsu''. In practice, ''shichidō garan'' often simply means a large temple with many buildings.{{cite book | last = Imaizumi | first = Yoshio | title = Nihon Bukkyo Shi Jiten | publisher = Yoshikawa Kobunkan | location = City | year = 1999 | isbn = 978-4-642-01334-5 }} ==Etymology and history of the term== {{nihongo|''Garan''|伽藍}}Also called {{nihongo|sōen|僧園}} {{nihongo|shūen|衆園}} and {{nihongo|shōja|精舎}} in Japanese is an abbreviated form of the expression {{nihongo|''sōgya ranma''|僧伽藍摩}}, itself a transliteration of the Sanskrit ''saMghaaraama'' (सँघाराम), literally meaning "garden for monks".JAANUS, ''garan'' A Japanese ''garan'' was originally just a park where monks gathered together with their teacher, but the term later came to mean "[[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]]". The word ''garan'' can be found in a record in [[Nihon Shoki]] dated 552, although no monastery of this time survives, so we don't know what they were like. The compound word {{nihongo|''shichidō garan''|七堂伽藍|seven hall temple}} is found in a much more recent literature of [[Edo period]],[[Nihon Kokugo Daijiten]] referring to a complex that had a complete set of buildings forming an ideal Buddhist temple. == Early ''garan'' layout == A record dated 577 in the [[Nihon Shoki]] states that a mission including among others a temple architect and a Buddhist image maker was sent by {{nihongo|[[Seong of Baekje|King Seong of Baekje]]|聖王}} to Japan, with more Buddhist related artisans sent over in the following years.{{cite book |author=Grayson, James Huntley |title=Korea: a religious history |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |location=London |year=2002 |pages=33 |isbn=0-7007-1605-X }} Excavations carried out between 1979-1980 on the temple site of [[Jeongnimsa]] in [[Buyeo County|Buyeo]], capital of [[Baekje]] from 538 to 663, revealed that the original temple was laid out in a typical north-to-south style with key buildings put on the center axis,{{cite web|url=http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|title=National Research Institute of Cultural Heritage|access-date=2010-06-30|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727120021/http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|archive-date=2011-07-27}} which was an arrangement closely adhered to at [[Shitennō-ji]] in [[Ōsaka]]. ==Composition of a ''shichidō garan''== What is counted in the group of seven buildings, or ''shichidō'', can vary greatly from temple to temple, from sect to sect, and from time to time. As mentioned above, ''shichidō garan'' could mean a complete temple or even simply a large temple complex. According to a 13th-century text,[[Shōtoku Taishi]] Denkokonmokurokushō (聖徳太子伝古今目録抄) "a garan is a temple with a ''hon-dō'' (main hall), a ''[[tō]]'' (pagoda), a ''kō-dō'' (lecture hall), a ''[[shōrō]]'' (belfry), a ''jiki-dō'' (refectory), a ''sōbō'' (monks' living quarters), and a ''[[kyōzō]]'' (scriptures deposit, library)." These are the seven listed as ''shichidō'' elements of a {{nihongo|[[Buddhism in Japan#Asuka and Nara Periods|Nanto Rokushū]]|南都六宗|Nara six sects}}The six Buddhist schools {{nihongo2|南都六宗}}, introduced to Japan during the [[Asuka Period|Asuka]] and [[Nara period]]s temple.Iwanami [[Kōjien]] A 15th-century text{{nihongo|Sekiso Ōrai|尺素往来}} describes how [[Zen]] school temples ([[Sōtō]] ({{lang|ja|曹洞}}), [[Rinzai]] ({{lang|ja|臨済}}))The [[Ōbaku|Ōbaku School]] ({{lang|ja|黃檗}}) arrived in Japan in the 17th century. included a ''[[butsuden]]'' or ''butsu-dō'' (main hall), a ''hattō'' (lecture hall), a ''kuin'' (kitchen/office), a ''sō-dō'' (building dedicated to [[Zazen]]), a ''[[sanmon]]'' (main gate), a ''tōsu'' (toilet) and a ''yokushitsu'' (bath). ===Loss of importance of the pagoda within the ''garan''=== [[File:飛鳥寺復元図.jpg|thumb|left|A reconstruction of [[Asuka-dera]]'s original layout with a pagoda at its center]] Because of the relics they contained, wooden pagodas used to be the centerpiece of the ''garan'', the seven edifices considered indispensable for a temple.*{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |edition=First |pages=40–41 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} They gradually lost importance and were replaced by the ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|kondō]]'' (golden hall), because of the magic powers believed to lie within the images the building housed. This loss of status was so complete that the Zen sects, which arrived late in Japan from China, normally do not build any pagoda at all. The layout of four early temples clearly illustrates this trend: they are in chronological order [[Asuka-dera]], [[Shitennō-ji]], [[Hōryū-ji]], and [[Yakushi-ji]]. In the first, the pagoda was at the very center of the ''garan'' surrounded by three small ''kondō'' (see the reconstruction of the temple's original layout). In the second, a single ''kondō'' is at the center of the temple and the pagoda lies in front of it. At Hōryū-ji, they are one next to the other. Yakushi-ji has a single, large ''kondō'' at the center with two pagodas on the sides. The same evolution can be observed in Buddhist temples in China. ==Examples of ''garan''== ===Hōryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Horyuji Temple.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Hōryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] {{nihongo|Hōryū-ji|法隆寺}} is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple of the "[[Shōtoku (sect)|Shōtoku]]" sect in [[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. Its ''garan'' is composed of (see plan on the right): A ''Chū[[Mon (architecture)|mon]]'' (中門)
In a temple, the gate after the ''naindaimon'' connected to a ''kairō'' B ''[[Kairō]]'' (回廊・廻廊)
A long and roofed [[portico]]-like passage surrounding the ''kondō'' and the pagoda.JAANUS entry of the same name C ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kon-dō]]'' (金堂)
The main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship. D ''[[Tō]]''
A pagoda, which is an evolution of the [[stupa]] (a kind of [[reliquary]]) . After reaching China, the stupa evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen). E ''Kōdō'' (講堂)
The lecture hall of a non-Zen ''garan''. F ''[[Kyōzō]]'' (経蔵)
Lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of [[sūtra]]s and books about the temple's history. Also called '''kyōdō'''. G ''[[Shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)
A belfry ===Zuiryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Zuiryuji.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Zuiryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] [[Zuiryū-ji (Toyama)|Zuiryū-ji]] is a Zen temple of the [[Sōtō]] sect in [[Takaoka, Toyama|Takaoka]], [[Toyama Prefecture]]. A ''[[Sōmon]]'' (総門)
The gate at the entrance of a temple. It precedes the bigger and more important ''sanmon''. B ''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門 or 山門)
The gate in front of the '''butsuden''', most commonly having two stories ([[nijūmon]]). The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''gate of the three liberations''. Its openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment. Entering, one can symbolically free him or herself from the three passions of {{nihongo|ton|貪| greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009 C ''Kairō'' (回廊)
See above D ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Butsuden]]'' (仏殿)
Lit. "Hall of Buddha". A building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a ''bodhisattva'' and dedicated to prayer. E ''Hōdō'' (法堂)
Lit. "[[Dharma]] hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the '''hō'''). F ''Zendō'' (禅堂)
Lit. "hall of Zen". The building where monks practice '''zazen''', and one of the main structures of a Zen '''garan'''. G ''Shōrō'' (鐘楼)
A belfry H ''Kuri'' (庫裏)
A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a Zen ''garan''. Another typical Zen ''garan'', of which [[Kenchō-ji]]'s is a good example, begins with the ''sōmon'' followed by the ''sanmon'', the main hall (the ''[[butsuden]]''), the lecture hall (''hattō''), and the chief abbot's residence (''hōjō'') all aligned more or less on a north to south axis, with the bath house (''yokushitsu'') and the [[sūtra]] repository (''[[kyōzō]]'') to its east, and the monks' hall (''sodō'') to its west. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Notes== ==References== * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 27, 2009 * Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{commons Category|Shichidō garan}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shichido garan}} [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shichidō garan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

In a temple, the gate after the ''naindaimon'' connected to a ''kairō'' B ''[[Kairō]]'' (回廊・廻廊)
A long and roofed [[portico]]-like passage surrounding the ''kondō'' and the pagoda.JAANUS entry of the same name C ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kon-dō]]'' (金堂)
The main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship. D ''[[Tō]]''
A pagoda, which is an evolution of the [[stupa]] (a kind of [[reliquary]]) . After reaching China, the stupa evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen). E ''Kōdō'' (講堂)
The lecture hall of a non-Zen ''garan''. F ''[[Kyōzō]]'' (経蔵)
Lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of [[sūtra]]s and books about the temple's history. Also called '''kyōdō'''. G ''[[Shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)
A belfry ===Zuiryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Zuiryuji.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Zuiryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] [[Zuiryū-ji (Toyama)|Zuiryū-ji]] is a Zen temple of the [[Sōtō]] sect in [[Takaoka, Toyama|Takaoka]], [[Toyama Prefecture]]. A ''[[Sōmon]]'' (総門)

The gate in front of the '''butsuden''', most commonly having two stories ([[nijūmon]]). The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''gate of the three liberations''. Its openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment. Entering, one can symbolically free him or herself from the three passions of {{nihongo|ton|貪| greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009 C ''Kairō'' (回廊)
See above D ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Butsuden]]'' (仏殿)
Lit. "Hall of Buddha". A building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a ''bodhisattva'' and dedicated to prayer. E ''Hōdō'' (法堂)
Lit. "[[Dharma]] hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the '''hō'''). F ''Zendō'' (禅堂)
Lit. "hall of Zen". The building where monks practice '''zazen''', and one of the main structures of a Zen '''garan'''. G ''Shōrō'' (鐘楼)
A belfry H ''Kuri'' (庫裏)
A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a Zen ''garan''. Another typical Zen ''garan'', of which [[Kenchō-ji]]'s is a good example, begins with the ''sōmon'' followed by the ''sanmon'', the main hall (the ''[[butsuden]]''), the lecture hall (''hattō''), and the chief abbot's residence (''hōjō'') all aligned more or less on a north to south axis, with the bath house (''yokushitsu'') and the [[sūtra]] repository (''[[kyōzō]]'') to its east, and the monks' hall (''sodō'') to its west. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Notes== ==References== * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 27, 2009 * Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{commons Category|Shichidō garan}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shichido garan}} [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Shichidō garan]][[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shichidō garan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{short description|Japanese Buddhist term}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2014}} {{redirect|Garan|people named Garan|Garan (name)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref04s5s4290.jpg|thumb|300px|Part of [[Tōshōdai-ji]]'s ''garan'' (left to right, the ''kondō'', the ''kōdō'', the ''korō'' and the ''raidō'')]] '''''Shichidō garan''''' is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] term indicating the seven [[Dō (architecture)|halls]] composing the ideal Buddhist temple compound. This compound word is composed of {{nihongo|''shichidō''|七堂|}}, literally meaning "seven halls", and {{nihongo|''garan''|伽藍}}, meaning "temple". The term is often shortened to just ''garan''. Which seven halls the term refers to varies, and 七堂 may be a misinterpretation of {{nihongo|''shitsudō''|悉堂}}, meaning "complete temple".Iwanami [[Kōjien]]Kōsetsu Bukkyō Daijiten (広説仏教語大辞典)The Japanese character 七 can also be pronounced ''shitsu''. In practice, ''shichidō garan'' often simply means a large temple with many buildings.{{cite book | last = Imaizumi | first = Yoshio | title = Nihon Bukkyo Shi Jiten | publisher = Yoshikawa Kobunkan | location = City | year = 1999 | isbn = 978-4-642-01334-5 }} ==Etymology and history of the term== {{nihongo|''Garan''|伽藍}}Also called {{nihongo|sōen|僧園}} {{nihongo|shūen|衆園}} and {{nihongo|shōja|精舎}} in Japanese is an abbreviated form of the expression {{nihongo|''sōgya ranma''|僧伽藍摩}}, itself a transliteration of the Sanskrit ''saMghaaraama'' (सँघाराम), literally meaning "garden for monks".JAANUS, ''garan'' A Japanese ''garan'' was originally just a park where monks gathered together with their teacher, but the term later came to mean "[[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]]". The word ''garan'' can be found in a record in [[Nihon Shoki]] dated 552, although no monastery of this time survives, so we don't know what they were like. The compound word {{nihongo|''shichidō garan''|七堂伽藍|seven hall temple}} is found in a much more recent literature of [[Edo period]],[[Nihon Kokugo Daijiten]] referring to a complex that had a complete set of buildings forming an ideal Buddhist temple. == Early ''garan'' layout == A record dated 577 in the [[Nihon Shoki]] states that a mission including among others a temple architect and a Buddhist image maker was sent by {{nihongo|[[Seong of Baekje|King Seong of Baekje]]|聖王}} to Japan, with more Buddhist related artisans sent over in the following years.{{cite book |author=Grayson, James Huntley |title=Korea: a religious history |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |location=London |year=2002 |pages=33 |isbn=0-7007-1605-X }} Excavations carried out between 1979-1980 on the temple site of [[Jeongnimsa]] in [[Buyeo County|Buyeo]], capital of [[Baekje]] from 538 to 663, revealed that the original temple was laid out in a typical north-to-south style with key buildings put on the center axis,{{cite web|url=http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|title=National Research Institute of Cultural Heritage|access-date=2010-06-30|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727120021/http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|archive-date=2011-07-27}} which was an arrangement closely adhered to at [[Shitennō-ji]] in [[Ōsaka]]. ==Composition of a ''shichidō garan''== What is counted in the group of seven buildings, or ''shichidō'', can vary greatly from temple to temple, from sect to sect, and from time to time. As mentioned above, ''shichidō garan'' could mean a complete temple or even simply a large temple complex. According to a 13th-century text,[[Shōtoku Taishi]] Denkokonmokurokushō (聖徳太子伝古今目録抄) "a garan is a temple with a ''hon-dō'' (main hall), a ''[[tō]]'' (pagoda), a ''kō-dō'' (lecture hall), a ''[[shōrō]]'' (belfry), a ''jiki-dō'' (refectory), a ''sōbō'' (monks' living quarters), and a ''[[kyōzō]]'' (scriptures deposit, library)." These are the seven listed as ''shichidō'' elements of a {{nihongo|[[Buddhism in Japan#Asuka and Nara Periods|Nanto Rokushū]]|南都六宗|Nara six sects}}The six Buddhist schools {{nihongo2|南都六宗}}, introduced to Japan during the [[Asuka Period|Asuka]] and [[Nara period]]s temple.Iwanami [[Kōjien]] A 15th-century text{{nihongo|Sekiso Ōrai|尺素往来}} describes how [[Zen]] school temples ([[Sōtō]] ({{lang|ja|曹洞}}), [[Rinzai]] ({{lang|ja|臨済}}))The [[Ōbaku|Ōbaku School]] ({{lang|ja|黃檗}}) arrived in Japan in the 17th century. included a ''[[butsuden]]'' or ''butsu-dō'' (main hall), a ''hattō'' (lecture hall), a ''kuin'' (kitchen/office), a ''sō-dō'' (building dedicated to [[Zazen]]), a ''[[sanmon]]'' (main gate), a ''tōsu'' (toilet) and a ''yokushitsu'' (bath). ===Loss of importance of the pagoda within the ''garan''=== [[File:飛鳥寺復元図.jpg|thumb|left|A reconstruction of [[Asuka-dera]]'s original layout with a pagoda at its center]] Because of the relics they contained, wooden pagodas used to be the centerpiece of the ''garan'', the seven edifices considered indispensable for a temple.*{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |edition=First |pages=40–41 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} They gradually lost importance and were replaced by the ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|kondō]]'' (golden hall), because of the magic powers believed to lie within the images the building housed. This loss of status was so complete that the Zen sects, which arrived late in Japan from China, normally do not build any pagoda at all. The layout of four early temples clearly illustrates this trend: they are in chronological order [[Asuka-dera]], [[Shitennō-ji]], [[Hōryū-ji]], and [[Yakushi-ji]]. In the first, the pagoda was at the very center of the ''garan'' surrounded by three small ''kondō'' (see the reconstruction of the temple's original layout). In the second, a single ''kondō'' is at the center of the temple and the pagoda lies in front of it. At Hōryū-ji, they are one next to the other. Yakushi-ji has a single, large ''kondō'' at the center with two pagodas on the sides. The same evolution can be observed in Buddhist temples in China. ==Examples of ''garan''== ===Hōryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Horyuji Temple.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Hōryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] {{nihongo|Hōryū-ji|法隆寺}} is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple of the "[[Shōtoku (sect)|Shōtoku]]" sect in [[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. Its ''garan'' is composed of (see plan on the right): A ''Chū[[Mon (architecture)|mon]]'' (中門)
In a temple, the gate after the ''naindaimon'' connected to a ''kairō'' B ''[[Kairō]]'' (回廊・廻廊)
A long and roofed [[portico]]-like passage surrounding the ''kondō'' and the pagoda.JAANUS entry of the same name C ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kon-dō]]'' (金堂)
The main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship. D ''[[Tō]]''
A pagoda, which is an evolution of the [[stupa]] (a kind of [[reliquary]]) . After reaching China, the stupa evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen). E ''Kōdō'' (講堂)
The lecture hall of a non-Zen ''garan''. F ''[[Kyōzō]]'' (経蔵)
Lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of [[sūtra]]s and books about the temple's history. Also called '''kyōdō'''. G ''[[Shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)
A belfry ===Zuiryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Zuiryuji.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Zuiryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] [[Zuiryū-ji (Toyama)|Zuiryū-ji]] is a Zen temple of the [[Sōtō]] sect in [[Takaoka, Toyama|Takaoka]], [[Toyama Prefecture]]. A ''[[Sōmon]]'' (総門)
The gate at the entrance of a temple. It precedes the bigger and more important ''sanmon''. B ''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門 or 山門)
The gate in front of the '''butsuden''', most commonly having two stories ([[nijūmon]]). The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''gate of the three liberations''. Its openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment. Entering, one can symbolically free him or herself from the three passions of {{nihongo|ton|貪| greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009 C ''Kairō'' (回廊)
See above D ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Butsuden]]'' (仏殿)
Lit. "Hall of Buddha". A building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a ''bodhisattva'' and dedicated to prayer. E ''Hōdō'' (法堂)
Lit. "[[Dharma]] hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the '''hō'''). F ''Zendō'' (禅堂)
Lit. "hall of Zen". The building where monks practice '''zazen''', and one of the main structures of a Zen '''garan'''. G ''Shōrō'' (鐘楼)
A belfry H ''Kuri'' (庫裏)
A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a Zen ''garan''. Another typical Zen ''garan'', of which [[Kenchō-ji]]'s is a good example, begins with the ''sōmon'' followed by the ''sanmon'', the main hall (the ''[[butsuden]]''), the lecture hall (''hattō''), and the chief abbot's residence (''hōjō'') all aligned more or less on a north to south axis, with the bath house (''yokushitsu'') and the [[sūtra]] repository (''[[kyōzō]]'') to its east, and the monks' hall (''sodō'') to its west. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Notes== ==References== * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 27, 2009 * Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{commons Category|Shichidō garan}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shichido garan}} [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Shichidō garan]][[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shichidō garan]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{short description|Japanese Buddhist term}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2014}} {{redirect|Garan|people named Garan|Garan (name)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref04s5s4290.jpg|thumb|300px|Part of [[Tōshōdai-ji]]'s ''garan'' (left to right, the ''kondō'', the ''kōdō'', the ''korō'' and the ''raidō'')]] '''''Shichidō garan''''' is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] term indicating the seven [[Dō (architecture)|halls]] composing the ideal Buddhist temple compound. These are the seven listed as ''shichidō'' elements of a {{nihongo|[[Buddhism in Japan#Asuka and Nara Periods|Nanto Rokushū]]|南都六宗|Nara six sects}}The six Buddhist schools {{nihongo2|南都六宗}}, introduced to Japan during the [[Asuka Period|Asuka]] and [[Nara period]]s temple.Iwanami [[Kōjien]] A 15th-century text{{nihongo|Sekiso Ōrai|尺素往来}} describes how [[Zen]] school temples ([[Sōtō]] ({{lang|ja|曹洞}}), [[Rinzai]] ({{lang|ja|臨済}}))The [[Ōbaku|Ōbaku School]] ({{lang|ja|黃檗}}) arrived in Japan in the 17th century. included a ''[[butsuden]]'' or ''butsu-dō'' (main hall), a ''hattō'' (lecture hall), a ''kuin'' (kitchen/office), a ''sō-dō'' (building dedicated to [[Zazen]]), a ''[[sanmon]]'' (main gate), a ''tōsu'' (toilet) and a ''yokushitsu'' (bath). ===Loss of importance of the pagoda within the ''garan''=== [[File:飛鳥寺復元図.jpg|thumb|left|A reconstruction of [[Asuka-dera]]'s original layout with a pagoda at its center]] Because of the relics they contained, wooden pagodas used to be the centerpiece of the ''garan'', the seven edifices considered indispensable for a temple.*{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |edition=First |pages=40–41 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} They gradually lost importance and were replaced by the ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|kondō]]'' (golden hall), because of the magic powers believed to lie within the images the building housed. Its ''garan'' is composed of (see plan on the right): A ''Chū[[Mon (architecture)|mon]]'' (中門)
In a temple, the gate after the ''naindaimon'' connected to a ''kairō'' B ''[[Kairō]]'' (回廊・廻廊)
A long and roofed [[portico]]-like passage surrounding the ''kondō'' and the pagoda.JAANUS entry of the same name C ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kon-dō]]'' (金堂)
The main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship. D ''[[Tō]]''
A pagoda, which is an evolution of the [[stupa]] (a kind of [[reliquary]]) . After reaching China, the stupa evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen). E ''Kōdō'' (講堂)
The lecture hall of a non-Zen ''garan''. F ''[[Kyōzō]]'' (経蔵)
Lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of [[sūtra]]s and books about the temple's history. Also called '''kyōdō'''. G ''[[Shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)
A belfry ===Zuiryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Zuiryuji.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Zuiryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] [[Zuiryū-ji (Toyama)|Zuiryū-ji]] is a Zen temple of the [[Sōtō]] sect in [[Takaoka, Toyama|Takaoka]], [[Toyama Prefecture]]. A ''[[Sōmon]]'' (総門)
The gate at the entrance of a temple. It precedes the bigger and more important ''sanmon''. B ''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門 or 山門)
The gate in front of the '''butsuden''', most commonly having two stories ([[nijūmon]]). The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''gate of the three liberations''. Its openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment. Entering, one can symbolically free him or herself from the three passions of {{nihongo|ton|貪| greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009 C ''Kairō'' (回廊)
See above D ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Butsuden]]'' (仏殿)
Lit. "Hall of Buddha". A building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a ''bodhisattva'' and dedicated to prayer. E ''Hōdō'' (法堂)

A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a Zen ''garan''. Another typical Zen ''garan'', of which [[Kenchō-ji]]'s is a good example, begins with the ''sōmon'' followed by the ''sanmon'', the main hall (the ''[[butsuden]]''), the lecture hall (''hattō''), and the chief abbot's residence (''hōjō'') all aligned more or less on a north to south axis, with the bath house (''yokushitsu'') and the [[sūtra]] repository (''[[kyōzō]]'') to its east, and the monks' hall (''sodō'') to its west. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Notes== ==References== * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 27, 2009 * Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{commons Category|Shichidō garan}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shichido garan}} [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Shichidō garan]][[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shichidō garan]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{short description|Japanese Buddhist term}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2014}} {{redirect|Garan|people named Garan|Garan (name)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref04s5s4290.jpg|thumb|300px|Part of [[Tōshōdai-ji]]'s ''garan'' (left to right, the ''kondō'', the ''kōdō'', the ''korō'' and the ''raidō'')]] '''''Shichidō garan''''' is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] term indicating the seven [[Dō (architecture)|halls]] composing the ideal Buddhist temple compound. This compound word is composed of {{nihongo|''shichidō''|七堂|}}, literally meaning "seven halls", and {{nihongo|''garan''|伽藍}}, meaning "temple". The term is often shortened to just ''garan''. Which seven halls the term refers to varies, and 七堂 may be a misinterpretation of {{nihongo|''shitsudō''|悉堂}}, meaning "complete temple".Iwanami [[Kōjien]]Kōsetsu Bukkyō Daijiten (広説仏教語大辞典)The Japanese character 七 can also be pronounced ''shitsu''. In practice, ''shichidō garan'' often simply means a large temple with many buildings.{{cite book | last = Imaizumi | first = Yoshio | title = Nihon Bukkyo Shi Jiten | publisher = Yoshikawa Kobunkan | location = City | year = 1999 | isbn = 978-4-642-01334-5 }} ==Etymology and history of the term== {{nihongo|''Garan''|伽藍}}Also called {{nihongo|sōen|僧園}} {{nihongo|shūen|衆園}} and {{nihongo|shōja|精舎}} in Japanese is an abbreviated form of the expression {{nihongo|''sōgya ranma''|僧伽藍摩}}, itself a transliteration of the Sanskrit ''saMghaaraama'' (सँघाराम), literally meaning "garden for monks".JAANUS, ''garan'' A Japanese ''garan'' was originally just a park where monks gathered together with their teacher, but the term later came to mean "[[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]]". The word ''garan'' can be found in a record in [[Nihon Shoki]] dated 552, although no monastery of this time survives, so we don't know what they were like. The compound word {{nihongo|''shichidō garan''|七堂伽藍|seven hall temple}} is found in a much more recent literature of [[Edo period]],[[Nihon Kokugo Daijiten]] referring to a complex that had a complete set of buildings forming an ideal Buddhist temple. == Early ''garan'' layout == A record dated 577 in the [[Nihon Shoki]] states that a mission including among others a temple architect and a Buddhist image maker was sent by {{nihongo|[[Seong of Baekje|King Seong of Baekje]]|聖王}} to Japan, with more Buddhist related artisans sent over in the following years.{{cite book |author=Grayson, James Huntley |title=Korea: a religious history |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |location=London |year=2002 |pages=33 |isbn=0-7007-1605-X }} Excavations carried out between 1979-1980 on the temple site of [[Jeongnimsa]] in [[Buyeo County|Buyeo]], capital of [[Baekje]] from 538 to 663, revealed that the original temple was laid out in a typical north-to-south style with key buildings put on the center axis,{{cite web|url=http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|title=National Research Institute of Cultural Heritage|access-date=2010-06-30|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727120021/http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|archive-date=2011-07-27}} which was an arrangement closely adhered to at [[Shitennō-ji]] in [[Ōsaka]]. ==Composition of a ''shichidō garan''== What is counted in the group of seven buildings, or ''shichidō'', can vary greatly from temple to temple, from sect to sect, and from time to time. As mentioned above, ''shichidō garan'' could mean a complete temple or even simply a large temple complex. According to a 13th-century text,[[Shōtoku Taishi]] Denkokonmokurokushō (聖徳太子伝古今目録抄) "a garan is a temple with a ''hon-dō'' (main hall), a ''[[tō]]'' (pagoda), a ''kō-dō'' (lecture hall), a ''[[shōrō]]'' (belfry), a ''jiki-dō'' (refectory), a ''sōbō'' (monks' living quarters), and a ''[[kyōzō]]'' (scriptures deposit, library)." These are the seven listed as ''shichidō'' elements of a {{nihongo|[[Buddhism in Japan#Asuka and Nara Periods|Nanto Rokushū]]|南都六宗|Nara six sects}}The six Buddhist schools {{nihongo2|南都六宗}}, introduced to Japan during the [[Asuka Period|Asuka]] and [[Nara period]]s temple.Iwanami [[Kōjien]] A 15th-century text{{nihongo|Sekiso Ōrai|尺素往来}} describes how [[Zen]] school temples ([[Sōtō]] ({{lang|ja|曹洞}}), [[Rinzai]] ({{lang|ja|臨済}}))The [[Ōbaku|Ōbaku School]] ({{lang|ja|黃檗}}) arrived in Japan in the 17th century. included a ''[[butsuden]]'' or ''butsu-dō'' (main hall), a ''hattō'' (lecture hall), a ''kuin'' (kitchen/office), a ''sō-dō'' (building dedicated to [[Zazen]]), a ''[[sanmon]]'' (main gate), a ''tōsu'' (toilet) and a ''yokushitsu'' (bath). ===Loss of importance of the pagoda within the ''garan''=== [[File:飛鳥寺復元図.jpg|thumb|left|A reconstruction of [[Asuka-dera]]'s original layout with a pagoda at its center]] Because of the relics they contained, wooden pagodas used to be the centerpiece of the ''garan'', the seven edifices considered indispensable for a temple.*{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |edition=First |pages=40–41 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} They gradually lost importance and were replaced by the ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|kondō]]'' (golden hall), because of the magic powers believed to lie within the images the building housed. This loss of status was so complete that the Zen sects, which arrived late in Japan from China, normally do not build any pagoda at all. The layout of four early temples clearly illustrates this trend: they are in chronological order [[Asuka-dera]], [[Shitennō-ji]], [[Hōryū-ji]], and [[Yakushi-ji]]. In the first, the pagoda was at the very center of the ''garan'' surrounded by three small ''kondō'' (see the reconstruction of the temple's original layout). In the second, a single ''kondō'' is at the center of the temple and the pagoda lies in front of it. At Hōryū-ji, they are one next to the other. Yakushi-ji has a single, large ''kondō'' at the center with two pagodas on the sides. The same evolution can be observed in Buddhist temples in China. ==Examples of ''garan''== ===Hōryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Horyuji Temple.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Hōryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] {{nihongo|Hōryū-ji|法隆寺}} is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple of the "[[Shōtoku (sect)|Shōtoku]]" sect in [[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. Its ''garan'' is composed of (see plan on the right): A ''Chū[[Mon (architecture)|mon]]'' (中門)
In a temple, the gate after the ''naindaimon'' connected to a ''kairō'' B ''[[Kairō]]'' (回廊・廻廊)
A long and roofed [[portico]]-like passage surrounding the ''kondō'' and the pagoda.JAANUS entry of the same name C ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kon-dō]]'' (金堂)
The main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship. D ''[[Tō]]''
A pagoda, which is an evolution of the [[stupa]] (a kind of [[reliquary]]) . After reaching China, the stupa evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen). E ''Kōdō'' (講堂)
The lecture hall of a non-Zen ''garan''. F ''[[Kyōzō]]'' (経蔵)
Lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of [[sūtra]]s and books about the temple's history. Also called '''kyōdō'''. G ''[[Shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)
A belfry ===Zuiryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Zuiryuji.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Zuiryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] [[Zuiryū-ji (Toyama)|Zuiryū-ji]] is a Zen temple of the [[Sōtō]] sect in [[Takaoka, Toyama|Takaoka]], [[Toyama Prefecture]]. A ''[[Sōmon]]'' (総門)
The gate at the entrance of a temple. It precedes the bigger and more important ''sanmon''. B ''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門 or 山門)
The gate in front of the '''butsuden''', most commonly having two stories ([[nijūmon]]). The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''gate of the three liberations''. Its openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment. Entering, one can symbolically free him or herself from the three passions of {{nihongo|ton|貪| greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009 C ''Kairō'' (回廊)
See above D ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Butsuden]]'' (仏殿)
Lit. "Hall of Buddha". A building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a ''bodhisattva'' and dedicated to prayer. E ''Hōdō'' (法堂)
Lit. "[[Dharma]] hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the '''hō'''). F ''Zendō'' (禅堂)
Lit. "hall of Zen". The building where monks practice '''zazen''', and one of the main structures of a Zen '''garan'''. G ''Shōrō'' (鐘楼)
A belfry H ''Kuri'' (庫裏)
A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a Zen ''garan''. Another typical Zen ''garan'', of which [[Kenchō-ji]]'s is a good example, begins with the ''sōmon'' followed by the ''sanmon'', the main hall (the ''[[butsuden]]''), the lecture hall (''hattō''), and the chief abbot's residence (''hōjō'') all aligned more or less on a north to south axis, with the bath house (''yokushitsu'') and the [[sūtra]] repository (''[[kyōzō]]'') to its east, and the monks' hall (''sodō'') to its west. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Notes== ==References== * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 27, 2009 * Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{commons Category|Shichidō garan}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shichido garan}} [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Shichidō garan]][[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shichidō garan]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{short description|Japanese Buddhist term}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2014}} {{redirect|Garan|people named Garan|Garan (name)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref04s5s4290.jpg|thumb|300px|Part of [[Tōshōdai-ji]]'s ''garan'' (left to right, the ''kondō'', the ''kōdō'', the ''korō'' and the ''raidō'')]] '''''Shichidō garan''''' is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] term indicating the seven [[Dō (architecture)|halls]] composing the ideal Buddhist temple compound. This compound word is composed of {{nihongo|''shichidō''|七堂|}}, literally meaning "seven halls", and {{nihongo|''garan''|伽藍}}, meaning "temple". The term is often shortened to just ''garan''. Which seven halls the term refers to varies, and 七堂 may be a misinterpretation of {{nihongo|''shitsudō''|悉堂}}, meaning "complete temple".Iwanami [[Kōjien]]Kōsetsu Bukkyō Daijiten (広説仏教語大辞典)The Japanese character 七 can also be pronounced ''shitsu''. In practice, ''shichidō garan'' often simply means a large temple with many buildings.{{cite book | last = Imaizumi | first = Yoshio | title = Nihon Bukkyo Shi Jiten | publisher = Yoshikawa Kobunkan | location = City | year = 1999 | isbn = 978-4-642-01334-5 }} ==Etymology and history of the term== {{nihongo|''Garan''|伽藍}}Also called {{nihongo|sōen|僧園}} {{nihongo|shūen|衆園}} and {{nihongo|shōja|精舎}} in Japanese is an abbreviated form of the expression {{nihongo|''sōgya ranma''|僧伽藍摩}}, itself a transliteration of the Sanskrit ''saMghaaraama'' (सँघाराम), literally meaning "garden for monks".JAANUS, ''garan'' A Japanese ''garan'' was originally just a park where monks gathered together with their teacher, but the term later came to mean "[[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]]". The word ''garan'' can be found in a record in [[Nihon Shoki]] dated 552, although no monastery of this time survives, so we don't know what they were like. The compound word {{nihongo|''shichidō garan''|七堂伽藍|seven hall temple}} is found in a much more recent literature of [[Edo period]],[[Nihon Kokugo Daijiten]] referring to a complex that had a complete set of buildings forming an ideal Buddhist temple. == Early ''garan'' layout == A record dated 577 in the [[Nihon Shoki]] states that a mission including among others a temple architect and a Buddhist image maker was sent by {{nihongo|[[Seong of Baekje|King Seong of Baekje]]|聖王}} to Japan, with more Buddhist related artisans sent over in the following years.{{cite book |author=Grayson, James Huntley |title=Korea: a religious history |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |location=London |year=2002 |pages=33 |isbn=0-7007-1605-X }} Excavations carried out between 1979-1980 on the temple site of [[Jeongnimsa]] in [[Buyeo County|Buyeo]], capital of [[Baekje]] from 538 to 663, revealed that the original temple was laid out in a typical north-to-south style with key buildings put on the center axis,{{cite web|url=http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|title=National Research Institute of Cultural Heritage|access-date=2010-06-30|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727120021/http://www.nricp.go.kr/eng/ArchitecturalHeritage/Documentation.jsp|archive-date=2011-07-27}} which was an arrangement closely adhered to at [[Shitennō-ji]] in [[Ōsaka]]. ==Composition of a ''shichidō garan''== What is counted in the group of seven buildings, or ''shichidō'', can vary greatly from temple to temple, from sect to sect, and from time to time. As mentioned above, ''shichidō garan'' could mean a complete temple or even simply a large temple complex. According to a 13th-century text,[[Shōtoku Taishi]] Denkokonmokurokushō (聖徳太子伝古今目録抄) "a garan is a temple with a ''hon-dō'' (main hall), a ''[[tō]]'' (pagoda), a ''kō-dō'' (lecture hall), a ''[[shōrō]]'' (belfry), a ''jiki-dō'' (refectory), a ''sōbō'' (monks' living quarters), and a ''[[kyōzō]]'' (scriptures deposit, library)." These are the seven listed as ''shichidō'' elements of a {{nihongo|[[Buddhism in Japan#Asuka and Nara Periods|Nanto Rokushū]]|南都六宗|Nara six sects}}The six Buddhist schools {{nihongo2|南都六宗}}, introduced to Japan during the [[Asuka Period|Asuka]] and [[Nara period]]s temple.Iwanami [[Kōjien]] A 15th-century text{{nihongo|Sekiso Ōrai|尺素往来}} describes how [[Zen]] school temples ([[Sōtō]] ({{lang|ja|曹洞}}), [[Rinzai]] ({{lang|ja|臨済}}))The [[Ōbaku|Ōbaku School]] ({{lang|ja|黃檗}}) arrived in Japan in the 17th century. included a ''[[butsuden]]'' or ''butsu-dō'' (main hall), a ''hattō'' (lecture hall), a ''kuin'' (kitchen/office), a ''sō-dō'' (building dedicated to [[Zazen]]), a ''[[sanmon]]'' (main gate), a ''tōsu'' (toilet) and a ''yokushitsu'' (bath). ===Loss of importance of the pagoda within the ''garan''=== [[File:飛鳥寺復元図.jpg|thumb|left|A reconstruction of [[Asuka-dera]]'s original layout with a pagoda at its center]] Because of the relics they contained, wooden pagodas used to be the centerpiece of the ''garan'', the seven edifices considered indispensable for a temple.*{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |edition=First |pages=40–41 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} They gradually lost importance and were replaced by the ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|kondō]]'' (golden hall), because of the magic powers believed to lie within the images the building housed. This loss of status was so complete that the Zen sects, which arrived late in Japan from China, normally do not build any pagoda at all. The layout of four early temples clearly illustrates this trend: they are in chronological order [[Asuka-dera]], [[Shitennō-ji]], [[Hōryū-ji]], and [[Yakushi-ji]]. In the first, the pagoda was at the very center of the ''garan'' surrounded by three small ''kondō'' (see the reconstruction of the temple's original layout). In the second, a single ''kondō'' is at the center of the temple and the pagoda lies in front of it. At Hōryū-ji, they are one next to the other. Yakushi-ji has a single, large ''kondō'' at the center with two pagodas on the sides. The same evolution can be observed in Buddhist temples in China. ==Examples of ''garan''== ===Hōryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Horyuji Temple.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Hōryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] {{nihongo|Hōryū-ji|法隆寺}} is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple of the "[[Shōtoku (sect)|Shōtoku]]" sect in [[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. Its ''garan'' is composed of (see plan on the right): A ''Chū[[Mon (architecture)|mon]]'' (中門)
In a temple, the gate after the ''naindaimon'' connected to a ''kairō'' B ''[[Kairō]]'' (回廊・廻廊)
A long and roofed [[portico]]-like passage surrounding the ''kondō'' and the pagoda.JAANUS entry of the same name C ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kon-dō]]'' (金堂)
The main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship. D ''[[Tō]]''
A pagoda, which is an evolution of the [[stupa]] (a kind of [[reliquary]]) . After reaching China, the stupa evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen). E ''Kōdō'' (講堂)
The lecture hall of a non-Zen ''garan''. F ''[[Kyōzō]]'' (経蔵)
Lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of [[sūtra]]s and books about the temple's history. Also called '''kyōdō'''. G ''[[Shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)
A belfry ===Zuiryū-ji=== [[File:Plan of Zuiryuji.jpg|thumb|250 px|Plan of Zuiryū-ji's ''shichidō garan'']] [[Zuiryū-ji (Toyama)|Zuiryū-ji]] is a Zen temple of the [[Sōtō]] sect in [[Takaoka, Toyama|Takaoka]], [[Toyama Prefecture]]. A ''[[Sōmon]]'' (総門)
The gate at the entrance of a temple. It precedes the bigger and more important ''sanmon''. B ''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門 or 山門)
The gate in front of the '''butsuden''', most commonly having two stories ([[nijūmon]]). The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''gate of the three liberations''. Its openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment. Entering, one can symbolically free him or herself from the three passions of {{nihongo|ton|貪| greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009 C ''Kairō'' (回廊)
See above D ''[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Butsuden]]'' (仏殿)
Lit. "Hall of Buddha". A building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a ''bodhisattva'' and dedicated to prayer. E ''Hōdō'' (法堂)
Lit. "[[Dharma]] hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the '''hō'''). F ''Zendō'' (禅堂)
Lit. "hall of Zen". The building where monks practice '''zazen''', and one of the main structures of a Zen '''garan'''. G ''Shōrō'' (鐘楼)
A belfry H ''Kuri'' (庫裏)
A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a Zen ''garan''. Another typical Zen ''garan'', of which [[Kenchō-ji]]'s is a good example, begins with the ''sōmon'' followed by the ''sanmon'', the main hall (the ''[[butsuden]]''), the lecture hall (''hattō''), and the chief abbot's residence (''hōjō'') all aligned more or less on a north to south axis, with the bath house (''yokushitsu'') and the [[sūtra]] repository (''[[kyōzō]]'') to its east, and the monks' hall (''sodō'') to its west. == See also == * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==Notes== ==References== * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 27, 2009 * Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{commons Category|Shichidō garan}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shichido garan}} [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]][[en:Shichidō garan]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Shichidō garan]][[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]][[en:Shichidō garan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shichidō garan]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{short description|Japanese Buddhist term}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2014}} {{redirect|Garan|people named Garan|Garan (name)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref04s5s4290.jpg|thumb|300px|Part of [[Tōshōdai-ji]]'s ''garan'' (left to right, the ''kondō'', the ''kōdō'', the ''korō'' and the ''raidō'')]] '''''Shichidō garan''''' is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] term indicating the seven [[Dō (architecture)|halls]] composing the ideal Buddhist temple compound. This compound word is composed of {{nihongo|''shichidō''|七堂|}}, literally meaning "seven halls", and {{nihongo|''garan''|伽藍}}, meaning "temple". The term is often shortened to just ''garan''. Which seven halls the term refers to varies, and 七堂 may be a misinterpretation of {{nihongo|''shitsudō''|悉堂}}, meaning "complete temple".Iwanami [[Kōjien]]Kōsetsu Bukkyō Daijiten (広説仏教語大辞典)The Japanese character 七 can also be pronounced ''shitsu''. In practice, ''shichidō garan'' often simply means a large temple with many buildings.{{cite book | last = Imaizumi | first = Yoshio | title = Nihon Bukkyo Shi Jiten | publisher = Yoshikawa Kobunkan | location = City | year = 1999 | isbn = 978-4-642-01334-5 }} ==Etymology and history of the term== {{nihongo|''Garan''|伽藍}}Also called {{nihongo|sōen|僧園}} {{nihongo|shūen|衆園}} and {{nihongo|shōja|精舎}} in Japanese is an abbreviated form of the expression {{nihongo|''sōgya ranma''|僧伽藍摩}}, itself a transliteration of the Sanskrit ''saMghaaraama'' (सँघाराम), literally meaning "garden for monks".JAANUS, ''garan'' A Japanese ''garan'' was originally just a park where monks gathered together with their teacher, but the term later came to mean "[[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]]". The word ''garan'' can be found in a record in [[Nihon Shoki]] dated 552, although no monastery of this time survives, so we don't know what they were like.
